coredata.dc:title: Comparison among OH, N and P functionalized carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanocomposites for food industry wastewater remediation
coredata.dc:creator: Camilli, Emiliano
Foglia, María Lucía
Bravo, Jonás Perez
Copello, Guillermo Javier
Villanueva, María Emilia
coredata.dc:description: TiO2 nanoparticles present limited efficiency under visible light in photocatalytic for food and agriculture wastewater remediation. Hence, the exploration of modifications to enhance their photocatalytic activity has been studied. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) have recently gained attention as functional coatings for TiO2, but a comparison between different types of CQDs has not been performed before. In this study, TiO2 nanoparticles coated with CQD treated with NaOH, HNO3, and H3PO4 were synthesized and named OHTiO2, NTiO2 and PTiO2. The materials were characterized for the photocatalytic degradation of glucose, representing jam industry wastewater and showed enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation when compared to bare TiO2 nanoparticles. The type of functional groups on the CQDs played a crucial role in determining the photocatalytic performance of the composites. In one case, they reduce the band gap, as observed in the NTiO2, while in another case, they mitigate the influence of interferents, as observed in the PTiO2. This research provides valuable insights into the design and optimization of functionalized quantum dots for photocatalytic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Advanced oxidation processes
TiO2 nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Food wastewater remediation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of the initial glucose concentration
Glucose degradation in the presence of interferences and in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Agencia Nacional de Promoción Científica y Tecnológica
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Agencia Nacional de Promoción Científica y Tecnológica
Agencia Nacional De Promoción Científica Y Tecnológica

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported with grants from Universidad Nacional de Luj\u00E1n (PI2+) and Agencia Nacional de Promoci\u00F3n Cient\u00EDfica y Tecnol\u00F3gica (PICT-2018-01731).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL spectra (intensity vs wavelength) of A) OHCQD, B) NCQD and C) PCQD with the excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm.
FT-IR spectra from 500 to 1800 cm−1 of (A) OHCQD, NCQD and PCQD and (B) TiO2, OHTiO2, NTiO2 and PTiO2.
Diffuse reflectance spectra (wavelength range of 200–800 nm) (A) and Kubelka-Munk transform of diffuse reflectance data (B) of TiO2, OHTiO2, NTiO2 and PTiO2.

(A) C/C0 in dark conditions (B) C/C0 vs pH (C) Glucose C/C0 vs concentration under sun-light (D) C/C0 vs glucose vs time. Assays were performed under solar-light (60.000 lx, with <10 % variation), the initial concentration of glucose solution was 1000 mg L−1 (except in C) and 10.0 ± 2.0 mg of uncoated or coated NP were added.

Glucose photocatalytic degradation in the presence of different scavengers. Assays were performed under solar-light (60.000 lx, with <10 % variation), the initial concentration of glucose was 1000 mg L−1 and the scavengers concentration was 1 mM in all the cases.

Photodegradation of glucose by NTiO2 in the presence of chloride, bicarbonate, nitrate and sulfate anions. Assays were performed under solar-light (60.000 lx, with <10 % variation), the initial concentration of glucose was 1000 g L−1, the concentration of the interferents were: NO3 −: 45 mg L−1, SO4 2−: 400 mg L−1, HCO3 −: 400 mg L−1 and Cl−, 350 mg L−1.
C/C0 of total organic carbon in the degradation of organic compounds in marmalade using tap or distilled water as a rinsing solution.

head.ce:title: Comparison among OH, N and P functionalized carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanocomposites for food industry wastewater remediation

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Universidad Nacional de Luján (UNLu), Departamento de Ciencias Básicas, Ruta 5 y 7, Luján, Buenos Aires, Argentina.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 nanoparticles present limited efficiency under visible light in photocatalytic for food and agriculture wastewater remediation. Hence, the exploration of modifications to enhance their photocatalytic activity has been studied. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) have recently gained attention as functional coatings for TiO2, but a comparison between different types of CQDs has not been performed before. In this study, TiO2 nanoparticles coated with CQD treated with NaOH, HNO3, and H3PO4 were synthesized and named OHTiO2, NTiO2 and PTiO2. The materials were characterized for the photocatalytic degradation of glucose, representing jam industry wastewater and showed enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation when compared to bare TiO2 nanoparticles. The type of functional groups on the CQDs played a crucial role in determining the photocatalytic performance of the composites. In one case, they reduce the band gap, as observed in the NTiO2, while in another case, they mitigate the influence of interferents, as observed in the PTiO2. This research provides valuable insights into the design and optimization of functionalized quantum dots for photocatalytic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The Food and Agriculture industry is one of the principal economic activities. Both industries are highly dependent on water and generate large volumes of residual wastewater, contributing to aquatic environment contamination. This wastewater is highly variable in terms of chemical and physical properties, depending on the quantity and type of processes involved. Therefore, residual water usually contains multiple chemical and biological contaminants in an environment characterized by high organic matter content, raising the effluent's chemical oxygen demand (COD) and biological oxygen demand (BOD). Food processing plant residues include soluble and insoluble carbohydrates in the presence of sulfites and a highly acidic matrix [1,2]. In particular, the wastewater of fruit and vegetable processing industries contains large amounts of organic matter with a BOD ranging from 860 to 3200 mg O2/dm3 and a COD ranging from 919 to 3700 mg O2/dm3. These effluents are usually treated with biological stabilization ponds. The retention time of wastewater in stabilization ponds can vary depending on several factors, system's design, the wastewater's characteristics, and the specific treatment plan. Typically, the wastewater retention time in stabilization ponds ranges from weeks to months [3].

Advanced oxidation processes that use photocatalytic techniques have emerged as a viable alternative to traditional water remediation processes [3–7]. Photocatalysis offers several advantages such as completely degrading organic matter into carbon dioxide and inorganic compounds without the risk of secondary contamination [8–11]. Among the available photocatalyzers, TiO2 based semiconductors are effective and low-cost with a photocatalytic activity that can be tuned by varying their surface area, crystallinity and doping elements [12–14]. However, the high band gap value of TiO2 nanoparticles (TiO2 NP) limits their efficiency under visible light (λ > 388 nm), which represents <5 % of the incident solar light being utilized in the oxidation process. This is one of the limiting factors in the expansion and implementation of heterogeneous photocatalysis developments. Furthermore, UV translucent and expensive photoreactors are needed. Nevertheless, numerous modification techniques have been researched to overcome this drawback to improve the photocatalytic activity and efficiency of TiO2 under solar-light irradiation [12].

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are a type of carbon nanomaterial that possess unique and intriguing properties. They have a size of <10 nm bearing either an amorphous or crystalline core. They are usually synthesized through bottom-up or top-down methods, involving different chemical, electrochemical or physical techniques [15–18]. CQDs are obtained from either carbohydrates or organic acids undergoing hydrothermal, solvothermal, chemical or microwave treatments during a typical bottom-up synthesis. The alternative, the top-down synthesis, is based on the carbonization of a variety of carbon-containing materials, from food waste to leaves and paper ash [19–24]. Indeed, the fact that CQDs can be synthesized from waste increases the eco-friendliness of the method, raising its economic and environmental sustainability. In addition, CQDs contain a high density of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which facilitate their functionalization. As a result, CQDs have found numerous applications across different fields (e.g., for chemical detection, bioimaging, and drug delivery) [19,25,26]. CQDs have also been used to coat TiO2 in order to enhance its photocatalytic activity [27,28]. Nevertheless, as far as we are concerned, the comparison between different CQDs types in pollutant degradation studies has not been explored yet.

In this work, we have demonstrated the successful synthesis and characterization of CQD functionalized with OH, N or P, which were used to coat TiO2 nanoparticles. The resulting composite materials exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activity under solar light irradiation compared to bare TiO2 nanoparticles. Our findings also suggest that the type of functional group plays a crucial role in determining the photocatalytic performance of the composites. This research not only contributes to the advancement of water purification technologies but also provides crucial insights into the design and optimization of functionalized CQDs for diverse photocatalytic applications, seeking to contribute novel insights into the field, helping us understand better how these functional groups can enhance the efficiency of TiO2 nanoparticles in photocatalysis. From solar energy conversion to water purification and air remediation, our work expands the possibilities for sustainable and effective solutions to environmental challenges.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), nitric acid (HNO3), phosphoric acid (H3PO4) and glucose (C6H12O6) were purchased from Anedra (Argentina). TiO2 nanoparticles (AEROXIDE TiO2 P-25, Evonik) were kindly donated by Evonik Degussa Argentina SA. Peanuts were purchased in a local store. All other reagents were of analytical grade.

OH functionalized CQD (OHCQD) were prepared through the carbonization of milled peanut shells at a temperature of 220 °C for 2 h in the presence of air. The resulting carbonized product was dispersed in deionized water, filtered, and centrifuged. The supernatant was dialyzed for 24 h in distilled water. This material did not exhibit fluorescence when irradiated with a 365 nm light (Fig. S1). Then, the sample was treated with a diluted solution of NaOH (1 × 10−5 M) as a passivating agent and evident fluorescence was achieved.

N and P functionalized CQDs (NCQD and PCQD) were prepared following the hydrothermal method. Three grams of milled peanut shells and 10.0 mL of 1 M HNO3 or 1 M H3PO4 were carbonized at 220 °C for 2 h. The resulting carbonized product was dispersed in deionized water, filtered, and centrifuged. The supernatant was dialyzed for 24 h in distilled water.

The CQDs solution concentration was obtained following the determination of dissolved solids. Briefly, a sample of quantum dots was transferred to a pre-weighed dish and evaporated to dryness until constant weight in an oven at 180 ± 2 °C and weighted. The oven Instrumentalia DHG-9053A was used for the analysis.

In order to prepare modified TiO2 nanoparticles, 0.6 g of the nanoparticles were mixed with 10.0 mL of the previously prepared OHCQD, NCQD or PCQD. The suspension was stirred for 1 h and then dried at 60 °C overnight. The obtained materials were named OHTiO2, NTiO2 and PTiO2.

In order to calculate the fluorescence quantum yield, the comparative method was used. Quantum yield of CQD was calculated using the following equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
Q x
=
Q r
m x
/
m r
η x
2
/
η r
2
where Q
x and Q
r refer to the quantum yield of carbon dots and reference respectively; m
x and m
r are slopes of the regression of fluorescence intensities on absorbances for five different concentrations of CQD and quinine sulfate respectively. η
x and η
r are the refractive indexes of the solvent used to disperse carbon quantum dots (water) and quinine sulfate solution respectively. The Q
r was taken as 0.54 and the η
r was taken as 1.33 [29].

Attenuated total reflectance Fourier Transform InfraRed (ATR-FTIR) spectra (diamond attenuated total reflectance) of uncoated and coated NP, were recorded using a Nicolet iS50 Advanced Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). ATR-FTIR spectra were recorded with 32 scans and a resolution of 1 cm−1. FT-Raman spectra were acquired with an excitation laser beam of 1064 nm, 0.25 W laser power, resolution of 4 cm−1, 400 scans. All samples were previously dried for 24 h at 60 °C to avoid water-related bands interference.

Samples were coated with a 20 nm gold layer before being analyzed using a Philips 505 SEM and EDS analyzer. The micrographs were taken at 200× magnification. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images of dried samples were taken using a Zeiss 109 TEM.

The pH-dependent surface-charging of solid particles is a common phenomenon and it affects the adsorption of ionic species from solution and physical properties of dispersions. To find the PZC of TiO2, OHTiO2, NTiO2 and PTiO2 the nanoparticles were exposed for 48 h to pH 2–11 previously boiled 10 mM NaCl solutions and then determined the new equilibrium pH of each nanoparticle.

Absorption spectra (UV–Vis diffuse reflectance measurements) were recorded with a Shimadzu UV2401 PC in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm to evaluate the optical properties of the materials. Barium sulfate was employed as a reference material (100 % reflectance measurement).
A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-5301PC) with quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length was used to record the PL spectra with an excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm.

The photocatalytic activities were evaluated by the decomposition of glucose as a model of organic matter under solar-light in Buenos Aires city from January to April. The irradiation intensity was measured in the beginning of the assays and it was 60.000 lx, with <10 % variation. Aqueous solutions of glucose (1000 mg L−1) were placed in a vessel, and 10.0 ± 2.0 mg of uncoated or coated NP were added. Prior to irradiation, the suspensions were stirred in the dark for about 30 min. The suspensions were kept under constant air-equilibrated conditions before and during illumination. After 10 h, 0.100 mL aliquots were sampled and centrifuged to remove particles. The supernatants were analyzed by recording the UV–Vis spectra and monitoring variations in the maximum band (505 nm). This assay was conducted under different conditions in order to evaluate NP performance such as different concentrations of glucose, different pH values (3.0, 7.0 and 11.0) and interaction times. In order to determine the degradation of glucose, the method of glucose oxidase was performed [30]. Vessels containing only the CQDs and the glucose solution were used as control.

The photodegradation experiment was repeated by preparing the glucose solution in water containing the maximum allowable concentrations of nitrate (NO3 −, 45 mg L−1), sulfate (SO4 2−, 400 mg L−1), bicarbonate (HCO3 −, 400 mg L−1) and chloride (Cl−, 350 mg L−1) to evaluate their interference in this process. The chosen catalyst was NTiO2 since it had the best performance.

As expected, during the photocatalytic process several kinds of active species, including hydroxyl radicals (•OH), holes (h+), and superoxide radicals (•O2 −), were generated. To detect the active species produced through the photodecomposition of glucose, another glucose photodegradation experiment was carried out with the addition of 1 mM methanol (CH3OH), 1 mM ammonium oxalate ((NH4)2C2O4) and 1 mM hydroquinone (C6H6O2) •OH, h+, and •O2 −, respectively.

In order to evaluate the performance of the materials in real matrices, the photodegradation experiments were performed in wastewater simulated solutions. Wastewater simulated solutions were prepared by adding 1 mg of homemade orange jam to 1 L of distilled water or 1 L of tap water. Total organic carbon analysis was performed using an Elementar TOC analyzer.

The methodologies used for the tap water physicochemical characterization were based on the following methods: pH: Standard methods 4500H
+
; Conductivity: Standard methods 2510; Dissolved solids: Standard methods 2540; Alkalinity: Standard methods 2320 B; Hardness: Standard methods 2340C; Chlorides: Standard methods 4500 Cl
−
B; Nitrates: Standard methods 4500 NO
3
−
B; Nitrites: Standard methods 4500 NO
2
−
B; Ammonia: Standard methods 4500 NH
3
F and Sulfates: Standard methods 4500 SO
4
2−
E [31].

All experiments and their corresponding measurements were conducted in triplicate under identical conditions and statistically analyzed by one-way ANOVA and Tukey comparison post-test if ANOVA p < 0.05.

We obtained carbon quantum dots using peanut shells as the starting material, which were usually disposed of as waste from the peanut industry. We used the hydrothermal carbonization approach to obtain N and P functionalized CQDs (NCQD and PCQD, respectively), whereas we used a classical carbonization method to obtain OH-functionalized CQDs (OHCQD). First, we used various techniques to characterize the three types of CQD, namely OHCQD, NCQD, and PQCD. The quantum yield was a measure of the efficiency of a material to convert absorbed photons into emitted photons. In this case, the quantum yield of OHCQD was 7.8 %. Similarly, the quantum yield of PCQD was 6.1 %, while NQCD had the highest quantum yield of 10.3 %. Furthermore, the analysis of dissolved solids revealed the concentration of each quantum dot material in the samples: OHCQD had 0.0820 %, NCQD had 0.11 %, and PCQD had 0.13 %.

Since CQDs have a distinctive ability to fluoresce, we analyzed the photoluminescence spectra of OHCQD, NCQD and PCQD as shown in Fig. 1 . In OHCQD (Fig. 1A) the emission spectra showed a strong emission intensity and a shoulder under the excitation of 330 nm at 436 nm and 370 nm respectively. The emission peak was shifted to longer wavelengths at excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 400 nm with a progressive reduction in intensity.

Fig. 1B showed the PL spectra of synthesized NCQD. The intensity of the emission peak initially increased with the increase of the excitation wavelength, reaching maximum intensity at 438 nm when the excitation wavelength was 340 nm. A gradual decrease in emission intensity was observed upon further increasing the excitation wavelength. This behavior can be attributed to the distribution of various surface states due to the presence of various surface organic groups on NCQDs [32]. Fig. 1C showed the PL emission spectra of PCQDs. The maximum emission wavelength shifted depending on the wavelength of the associated excitation, which can be ranging from 300 to 400 nm; the maximum PL intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm with an emission wavelength of 366 nm [33].

The emission of all the samples varied when the excitation wavelength changed, indicating an excitation-dependent PL behavior from 300 to 400 nm. Many factors can affect the excitation-dependent fluorescence of CQDs. The size distribution of the CQDs is an important consideration since different sizes can have unique fluorescence behaviors. Furthermore, the composition of the CQDs, surface functional groups, and defects can affect how fluorescence responds to different excitation wavelengths. The quantum confinement effect, in which the size and structure of CQDs affect their electronic characteristics, is frequently linked to the excitation-dependent fluorescence. FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ) revealed the presence of functional groups such as –OH and –COOH on the surface of CQDs, confirming the existence of numerous trap states and heterogeneous chemical structures. These functional groups and emission traps were responsible for CQDs fluorescence and excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior since specific emission sites could be excited at specific wavelengths [34]. Furthermore, the quantum confinement effect was the most generally accepted phenomenon leading to excitation-dependent behavior.

Figs. 2A and S2A, showed CQDs FTIR analysis, which confirmed the functional groups present in the materials. OHCQD and NCQD spectra exhibited similar bands such as stretching vibration of CO at 1592 cm−1 and stretching vibrations of CC and CO at 875 cm−1and 1098 cm−1, respectively. However, the main differences between these samples were the characteristics of the bands between 4000 and 2700 cm−1. In OHCQD a broader and less intense band was observed where a peak at 3491 cm−1, assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, and a shoulder at 3130 cm−1 corresponding to CH stretching was found. In NCQD, we observed a more intense band at 3369 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH [32]. These results provided evidence of the different chemical characteristics of these CQDs. The spectrum of the PCQD was quite different, broad bands were found at 856 cm−1, 954 cm−1 corresponding to P-O-C stretching, 1098 cm−1 corresponding to PO stretching and 2290 cm−1 corresponding to PH stretching [35]. These hydrophilic groups were in congruence with the high hygroscopicity and clearly confirmed phosphorus doping in PCQD.

The pristine and coated nanoparticles were also characterized. Fig. 1B and S2B showed that the spectra for TiO2 and OHTiO2 were similar showing peaks at 3322 cm−1, assigned to the stretching vibrations of the OH, at 1605 cm−1, assigned to the bending vibrations of the adsorbed water molecules, and at 1400 cm−1 corresponding to CH. The spectrum of NTiO2 was quite similar, but the 1400 cm−1 peak disappeared and a shift of the other peak was observed towards 1650 cm−1. In the PTiO2 spectrum, we observed a band at 1605 cm−1, like the other spectra. The band at 960 cm−1 corresponds to P-O-C stretching, verifying the presence of PCQD.

The materials were also characterized using SEM, and no morphological differences were found between the samples. In the SEM-EDX analysis (Fig. S3A), we observed that the element found in the highest proportion was O, followed by Ti, for all the samples except for PTiO2, where the second most abundant element was P. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) mapping (Fig. S3B) revealed the homogeneous distribution of P in PTiO2. However, the distribution of N in the NTiO2 could not be characterized due to the weak response of this element using this technique. Figure SI2 showed the TEM images of nude and treated TiO2 nanoparticles. Nanoparticles were spherical and polydisperse with a layer around the nanoparticles which corresponded to the CQDs. The sizes of the nanoparticles were listed in Table S1. The morphology of the materials was studied before and after the photocatalysis and no difference was observed (Fig. S5).

These CQDs combined with TiO2 were characterized by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS). Fig. 3 showed that all nanoparticles presented light absorption capacity mainly in the UV region (λ < 400 nm), with higher absorbance observed in PTiO2. The Kubelka-Munk remission function determined band gap as:
(2)
F
R ∞
=
R ∞
1 −
R ∞
2
/ 2 , where
R ∞
=
R sample
/
R Standard

The indirect band gap was obtained by plotting [αhν]1/2 as a function of energy [eV] and linearly extrapolating the linear portion, where α was the absorption coefficient (F(R∞)) h was Planck's constant and ν was the incident photon frequency. The following indirect band gaps were obtained (Fig. 3b): E
g TiO2: 3.08 eV, E
g OHTIO2: 2.92 eV, E
g NTiO2: 2.83 eV and E
g PTiO2: 2.89 eV. This test aimed to examine the spectrum of light absorption by the catalyst and ascertain whether there is a shift in the activation band towards the visible light range caused by the presence of CQDs. When TiO2 was combined with CQDs, especially with NCQD, their band gap decreased, indicating a moderate shift in light absorption from the ultraviolet to the visible region of the spectrum.

Initially, all assays were conducted under dark conditions for all scenarios, and it was observed that both coated and uncoated TiO2 nanoparticles exhibited no capacity for adsorbing glucose (Fig. 4 A). Consequently, the elimination of glucose was solely attributed to photocatalytic degradation. Subsequently, we tested the various nanoparticles under different experimental conditions.

The medium pH impact on glucose degradation by coated and uncoated NP was depicted in Fig. 4B. The analysis of the variations of C/C0 as a function of pH showed three distinct patterns of behavior. In the case of TiO2 and NTiO2 the lowest C/C0 was observed at pH 3, and no significant differences were observed between pH 7 and 11. OHTiO2 presented a similar C/C0 at pH 3 and 11 and a higher value at pH 7. In the case of PTiO2, C/C0 increased along with pH. At pH 3, no significant differences were observed among the TiO2, OHTiO2, NTiO2 and PTiO2 (p > 0.05). At pH 7, NTiO2 C/C0 was significantly lower than when using the other catalysts (p < 0.05). At pH 11, significant differences were observed among all treatments except for NTiO2 and OHTiO2. The best performance was observed when using OHTiO2 and NTiO2 as a catalyst probably due to their lower indirect band gap values in comparison with TiO2. When using PTiO2 as a catalyst no degradation could be evidenced.

The pH influence in the photocatalytic degradation process may have multiple roles. First, the pH influences the ionization state of the catalyst surface. The point of zero charge (PZC) of TiO2 is at pH 7.05, OHTiO2 is at pH 7.07, NTiO2 is at pH 7.21 and PTiO2 is at pH 1.48. When the value of pH was lower than the PZC, the nanoparticle's surface presented a positive charge density and at higher values, it was negatively charged. The PZC of TiO2, OHTiO2, and NTiO2 were quite similar so that the surface would be charged similarly at different pH values. However, PTiO2 is negatively charged at all three pH values. Although glucose is a molecule that did not have ionizable groups and did not undergo direct changes in its structure or charge due to pH changes, the decrease in glucose degradation with increasing pH value in the case of PTiO2 may be due to the increase in the negative charge of the surface. In this more alkaline solution, repulsion between the negative charged surface of photocatalyst and the hydroxide anions possibly took place and might have prevented the formation of •OH and thus decreased glucose degradation [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the initial glucose concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Initial glucose concentrations were varied in the range of 250–1000 mg L−1 and the results were exposed in Fig. 3C. As it could be observed, the C/C0 increased along with the initial glucose concentration. This effect was possibly due to the saturation of active sites in the catalyst. At all tested initial concentrations, we have observed that the degradation is significantly higher when using CQD-treated catalysts compared to bare TiO2, with no significant differences observed among OHTiO2, NTiO2, and PTiO2 (p > 0.05). When TiO2 was employed as a catalyst, glucose degradation was lower and similar at all initial glucose concentrations. However, when using NTiO2, OHTiO2 and PTiO2, more significant degradation was observed when the initial concentration was 250 mg L−1, while no significant changes were observed between 500 and 1000 mg L−1.

The Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) kinetics model was commonly utilized to describe the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants (Eq. (3)).
(3)
r =
dC dt
=
kKC
1 + KC

In this equation, r represented the degradation rate of the reactant (mg L−1 min), C denoted the glucose concentration (mg L−1), t signified the illumination time, k represented the reaction rate constant (mg L−1 min), and K was the adsorption coefficient of the reactant (l/mg). When the initial analyte concentration, C
0 , was in millimolar solution, the equation could be simplified to an apparent first-order equation (Eq. (4)):
(4)
ln
Co C
= kKt =
k app
t

The plot of ln(C0/C) versus time depicted a linear function with a slope representing the apparent first-order rate constant, k
app . Studies carried out at low concentrations of pollutants were often well-suited to be described by first-order kinetics, and numerous research used this kinetic model. [38–40].

The obtained parameters (Table 1 ) reveal that the k
app and, consequently, the reaction rate varied depending on the catalyst employed. The k
app values exhibited the following sequence: OHTiO2 < TiO2 < PTiO2 < NTiO2, indicating an elevated photocatalytic activity in samples with higher k
app values. The enhanced photocatalytic activity observed in NCQD might be attributed to its lower band gap value.

The mechanisms involved in the photocatalysis mediated by TiO2 were previously described elsewhere [37,38]. The roles of the main active species such as h+, •OH, and O2 •− were investigated using different types of active species scavengers. Fig. 5 showed the photocatalytic activities of TiO2, OHTiO2, NTiO2 and PTiO2 in the degradation of glucose under the presence of methanol, ammonium oxalate and hydroquinone, which scavenged •OH, h+ and O2 •−, respectively.

When analyzing the results obtained when using bare TiO2 and NTiO2 as a catalyst, we did not observe significant differences among the different treatments, meaning that probably •OH, h+ and O2 •− were involved in the photocatalytic mechanism. In the presence of OHTiO2 as a catalyst, the h+ specie played a significant role in the photocatalytic mechanism. This was evident from the higher C/C0 observed in the presence of ammonium oxalate as compared to other conditions. For the PTiO2 the main involved species were h+ and •OH, since in the presence of methanol and ammonium oxalate the C/C0 was significantly higher than in the other condition.

The fact that •OH was one of the species involved in the mechanism of photocatalysis of PTiO2 reinforced the previously exposed theory that at higher pH values the formation of •OH from OH− was lower due to electrostatic repulsion between OH− and the negatively charged nanoparticles, decreasing glucose degradation in that condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose degradation in the presence of interferences and in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of inorganic anions could potentially affect the efficiency of photocatalysis in removing pollutants in several ways [39]. As an example, anions could compete with target pollutants for adsorption on the catalyst's surface, leading to a decline in the rate of pollutant degradation. Additionally, anions could scavenge •OH or other oxidizing species and may also undergo transformations into active radicals or create an electrostatic field near the catalyst, thereby facilitating the separation of electrons and holes and promoting the photocatalytic process.

In the experiment depicted in Fig. 6 , the impact of sulfate, bicarbonate, nitrate, and chloride on glucose degradation was evaluated under neutral conditions using NTiO2. The different anions had distinct impacts on the degradation of glucose on the three systems. In the case of HCO3 − and Cl−, we did not find a significant difference when compared to the control. However, SO4 2+ and NO3 − had a negative effect on the photocatalytic performance. The observed detrimental effect of NO3 −and SO4 2+ on photocatalytic degradation of glucose might be due to their ability to scavenge oxidizing holes and radicals.

Eqs. (5), (6) and (7) showed the reaction of SO4 2− with h+ and •OH. The reaction inhibition was a result of formed SO4 •- which is less reactive than •OH and h+ due to the resonance and larger size [32]. SO4 •- can also react with electrons to give sulfate anion. According to the literature, this behavior may also be caused by Cl− ions. However, when SO4 2− ions were present, the photocatalytic reaction occurred at a slower rate compared to when Cl− ions were present. This was because the adsorption of SO4 2− ions was higher due to their larger size and two valence anions, which increased competition for adsorption [40].
(5)
SO 4
2 −
+
h +
→
SO 4
• −
(6)
SO 4
2 −
+
•
OH →
SO 4
• –
+
OH −
(7)
SO 4
• –
+
e −
→
SO 4
2 −

It was reported that at a very low concentration (below 0.1 mM), nitrate anions could enhance the reaction rate of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants [34] as a result of the photolysis of nitrate anions. At higher concentrations, as in this work, we observed a decrease in the photocatalysis [41,42]. In the excess of nitrate anions, a competitive adsorption to the catalyst could occur, decreasing the glucose degradation. Once again, the inhibition was much higher in comparison to that of Cl− due to the larger size of anions that lead to a more effective blockage of the catalyst surface [42]. Nitrate anions could also react with positive holes and hydroxyl radicals acting as h+ and radical scavengers (Eqs. (8) and (9)) [43].
(8)
NO 3
−
+
h +
→
NO 3
•
(9)
NO 3
−
+
•
OH →
NO 3
•
+
OH −

The degradation of organic compounds in jam solutions using tap and distilled water was evaluated and the results of total organic carbon after sunlight exposure were shown in Table 2 . The results revealed distinct performance between the two water sources, with significantly better degradation observed in distilled water compared to tap water.

In the distilled water samples, a notable reduction in the concentration of organic compounds was observed when using NTiO2 and PTiO2. This could be attributed to the absence of interfering compounds and the lower band gap value in the case of NTiO2 and the PZC of PTiO2, which is at pH 1.48. The negatively charged surface of the catalyst at all working pH levels, also enables electrostatic repulsion against interferents.

Conversely, the degradation process of organic compounds in tap water was not significant. The presence of interfering compounds (shown in Table S2), such as nitrate and sulfate, appeared to interfere with the degradation of the pollutant, as previously established in this work.

This study successfully demonstrated the synthesis and characterization of TiO2 nanoparticles coated with different types of functionalized CQDs. The composite materials exhibited improved photocatalytic activity for the degradation of glucose under visible light irradiation compared to bare TiO2 nanoparticles. Furthermore, this research found that the type of functional group present on the CQDs significantly influenced the photocatalytic performance of the composites. These findings highlight the potential of functionalized quantum dots as electron reservoirs, which can aid in electron transfer and reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs. By utilizing CQDs derived from waste materials, this research further enhances the eco-friendliness and sustainability of the photocatalytic process. Overall, this research contributes to a better understanding of functionalized quantum dots as effective coatings for TiO2 in the context of water remediation.

Emiliano Camilli: Writing – original draft, Investigation. María Lucía Foglia: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Jonás Perez Bravo: Investigation. Guillermo Javier Copello: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. María Emilia Villanueva: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported with grants from Universidad Nacional de Luján (PI2+) and Agencia Nacional de Promoción Científica y Tecnológica (PICT-2018-01731).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111103.
